transcript.transcript: July 7, 2007. This is an interview with Julie Kreisenseck. This is taking place in the Congregational Church in Boise. OK, Julie. OK. Were you just saying that you were born in Boise? Yes, I was born in Boise. My father was born in Boise in 1884. Oh, 1884. Both his parents had been here. Am I allowed to ask you what year you were born? What year I was born? 1925. And what are your first memories of Julia Davis Park? Well, probably the lagoon and the swings. Tell me about the lagoon. Is it in the same place it is today? Yes, the lagoon is right where it is. I think it was deeper and Because we were always being told to be careful if we were out in boats, and I think now Practically weighed the whole thing But it had to be a special occasion because this was, you know, this was Depression time and it was a special occasion when we could go out in a boat. You had to pay a fee. Yes, and of course an older child went with us to make sure we behaved or until we got to be the older child that could go with other people. What about in the winter? Well, it didn't freeze very often. I do know other people skated, But I never had skates. So I know there were winters when people could go and skate and scrape the snow off. And they'd go and skate. But I don't remember. I was not involved with that. My main involvement with the park was just using the swings. And where were the swings? Well, I was going to say right where they are now, but they aren't there anymore. By the road that is between the main part of the park and the river, and they were north of that road, over by where the zoo has spread out to. And I do remember that the things where the zoo has spread to weren't there. I've got a lousy memory, Marilyn. I really do. But I remember thinking a few years ago that there was a pond there where the birds are now. Yeah, well, it was a bigger pond. And everybody could get to it. It wasn't enclosed around it. So you could go and I never was allowed to go put my feet in it and things, but I'm sure other people probably did. Now, did you live near the park? No. No. Where did you live? Well, until I was 5 and 1 1, I lived on the corner of Washington Street and 20th, where 20th runs into Washington. It just kind of curves there. And then We moved out to the last block on 15th Street, North 15th. Uh-huh. OK. So did you do it? So getting down to the park was not something that I just did. There were a lot of kids, particularly the boys, would ride their bikes down, you know. But I never did that. My friends and I just didn't do that sort of thing. I never went to the park without, as a child, without being with my family. And we'd go picnic. Ah. And you'd have like a, they had picnic tables? Yes, they had picnic tables. And on Sundays, the city band played. And so we would go listen to the city band. And did they have uniforms? Oh, they just wore black jackets and pants and white shirts. I think the men probably also wore ties, but they may not have in the middle of the summer. And where did they play? They played in the band show. The same band show we have now. I don't know when. You probably know more about when that was built than I do. You have it, but I don't. Yeah, well, it's somewhere. But I just remember it being there and there were celebrations, you know if it was a Memorial Day or Fourth of July or something things went on at the band show during the day. People spoke from there. And did you play an instrument when you were in school? Yes, I started playing flute when I was in the fifth grade, that my cousin Jim Hopper played clarinet and he was two years older than me. And the city band did not allow women to be part of it except there was one woman who was a member. She played trumpet. And I don't remember her name. In those days, we would just say she was a manly-looking woman. And it was perfectly acceptable that she played in the band. But no other women played in it. Now, my cousin Jim would play. And we knew quite a few people who played just as after he got into high school. And so the whole family would turn out to listen to the band. And when World War II started. And did you try and get into the band? Oh no, you knew you couldn't. It wasn't an option. And so when World War II started, well then they lost quite a few members. And I was invited to join the band, as well as a friend of mine who played clarinet. She and I played duets and things together in school. So that was just in probably 1942 and 43. And I stayed in Boise the year between high school and college. So the year of 44, I also played. So you went for like three years? No, two and a half, three years. I don't think we ever did much of anything except play the city park. And I don't even remember playing in a parade. I think when the war started, we probably just didn't have very many parades. No Because I wasn't a member of the Union The Union people got paid And we also played for the rodeos, which was my joy. Because I could play piccolo. And if I knew what key they were playing in, I didn't have to look at my music. I could watch the cowboys and the horses and everything and I could just sit there and tweedle-deedly around on some chords and nobody would know the difference because nobody was really listening to us anyhow. Well, they did at the opening. Where was the rodeo held? Oh, the rodeo was at the fairgrounds. And the fairgrounds were on the corner of Orchard and Fairview. What was it like when you joined the band at Julia Davis? Was it the men all welcomed you? Yeah, the men welcomed us. There wasn't any uncomfortableness. I was always such a naive person anyhow. I probably wouldn't have noticed. I just thought, oh, well, that's the way they are, and not paid any attention to that. But no, we just played. Was the other woman in there still in the band? Yes. Yes. And I should remember her name, but I don't. I mean, She played in other things in town, too. We had a symphony then. It was just starting, the very early starts of the Boise Philharmonic. And I don't remember whether she played in that or not. That's about all I got to say. That's all you had to say? Yeah, except that I loved the swings. And they had teeter-totters. And my cousin Jim really enjoyed holding me up in the air because he was bigger than I was. Just Jim Hopper? Yes, my cousin Jim Hopper. He'd get on one end and he'd get me up and not get off or not let me down. Yeah, that was scary. No, it was just irritating. Oh. I wanted to go up and down, and he just teased. Did you, were there, After you went away to college, then you came home? Well, no. I wasn't in town much after that. When did you come back to Boise? In 53. And I left in 43. Yeah, I guess in the fall of 44 I went away to school. And then I got married and lived elsewhere. And then you've lived here ever since 1953. Did you lose the park at all after 1953? Oh, I took my kids there, but not a lot. And I sat in the swings then. The swings were really high, Marilyn. I've heard other people say that. And you could swing and get way high. And as an adult, I still enjoyed Sneaking in tines to go down. I was really irritated when they took them out. Do you know why they took them out? Oh, I think they were worried about people getting hurt. I mean, I'm sure that's the only reason I can think of. So... Kids would swing high and jump, you know? Uh-huh, jump off. Uh-huh. And my children enjoyed them. They'd stand. If anybody was in the playground part of the park, somebody was pushing somebody or pumping in the swings. They were a popular part. They had a merry-go-round, too. And it was kid-powered, run along and push. And different kids would jump off and push. And then they'd get on and somebody else would jump off and push. You know, it wasn't very big. I'm not good at defining size, but maybe 10 kids size. When you came back to town, there was a band during part of that time, was there? There probably was, but I don't, well I'm sure there was, because I... Did Jim Hopper play in that, this week? Yeah, Jimmy would have been playing, because he was back from school and the war and everything by then. Do you have any memorabilia from those days? If I do, it's packed away where I can't get out of. I don't... Uh-huh. I... I know there's a picture of Jim somewhere. In the band? Well, I can just see him standing in the picture with his clarinet and dressed in black with a white shirt on. And he was still a young Yusef Ali was still in high school, probably. But Other than that, I can't think of any pictures. Did you have to practice? Oh, yes. Where did you practice? I have no idea. But you did practice. I don't think we practiced very often, now that you mention it. The concerts were on Sunday afternoon. Sunday afternoon. Did a lot of people come? Yeah. For Boise. We never felt like nobody was listening to us. Of course, if there was a summer rainstorm, we usually, I think, usually the band just sort of sat it out back, but people would scatter. Were there, was the park considered, when you were young going there, was it considered a safe place? Oh, yes. Never thought of it not being a safe place. And do you use the park at all now? Yes. I attend things that are going on there. I go to the zoo. I drive through it. Rather than taking Capitol Boulevard north and turning on Myrtle, if I've been to the library, I'll sometimes just go. And I go both ways. Sometimes I go around by the river. I enjoy the park. Uh-huh. To look at it. Yeah. One of the things that a year ago, a year and a half ago, I attended some of the lectures put on by the historical Huttsack group that does city history walks. And they did one down by the river, through the park, and mostly at the park, but some on the west side of 9th, over in there. And they were talking about all the trees and well there weren't trees along the river No, just a few it wasn't heavily wooded like it is now and you could just walk there wasn't any Bank, you just walked right down to the river and sat down and put your feet in There at the park It has changed its its relationship to the river So this was from Julia Davis. Yeah, just walk right Just walk right down to the river. Did people float down the river there? They may have, but it wasn't a thing. You know, there were probably, But inner tubes weren't that floating. Having inner tubes to float on, they belonged in your car, you know? Yeah. But it could have been mostly people fished in the river rather than, and I know people fished at the park, too. And of course, the Capital River Bridge wasn't built when I was a child. Oh, really? Yeah. There was the 8th Street Bridge. Yeah, that one that you can walk over now. Oh, yeah, sure. And where the Cottonwood Grill is now, was there a park there then? No. There had been at one time, but it was like an amusement park, a paid park. There was? Apparently so. I just read about it. But it wasn't part of Julia Davis. It must have been short-lived. The Capitol Boulevard, of course, was there. And 7th Street was there, yeah. So, but how, but if you went from the depot. No you went over on 8th street. 8th street okay. Yeah. Okay. And you know in those days the thing to do on a hot night was go up to the Depot. Uh-huh. In the summer? Mm-hmm. Look at the city lights? Yeah, and the pool, and the fish, and the grotto, and the harbor, and all of that. So Julia Davis was just about the only park. Well, municipal park also. I don't know what its years are, but if you wanted to have something to cook on, you went to Municipal Park. There wasn't anything in Julia Davis Park. And then I could be wrong, and somebody could say, yes, there was, Julie. And I'd say, well, I just don't remember it. That's all right. That's all right. I know that the boys that were my age spent a lot of time in the river, various places, fishing or swimming and things. But some of it was over by the Trestle Bridge, which is down where about 340th, you know, much further west. But you get kids anywhere, they're going to head for water. It was mostly boys. Well, yeah. I mean, as far as I know, because I was much more of a cultured, behave yourself little girl. I was not one that had a lot of time just out wandering. Not that I wouldn't have enjoyed it, but I didn't even know it went on. And was that something that more boys were roaming around. Right. Girls were homeless. Yeah. So now you still use the park for many generations then. Yes. My daughter Donna, who still lives in Boise, I'm sure goes to the park for art in the park. I know she goes for Art in the Park. She's a working woman, so she doesn't have a lot of time to go. But when there's something going on there that you're interested in, she's going to go. Probably Earth's birthday, which is coming up in April. Uh-huh. That's it, huh? That's it, Marilyn. I told you this was not a lot. Well, I think it's very interesting. And you don't, I've just always wondered about the band. Do you not remember who the band leader was? Oh, no. I can't. That's all right. But it was in the papers I'm sure his I mean if you said the name I would know. I can visualize him. He was roundish. But everybody knew who he was. I'm just terrible with names. Well, that's all right. But it was a city band. It was the Boise City Band. Played by the city. And I asked Jan Newell, who was here at the meeting prior to you and I getting together, her husband is Bob Newell, and he played in the city band. I called to ask him, but he wasn't home. And so Jan asked him, and he said, yes, he played, but it was a long time ago. He's a trombone player. Because he grew up in Boise and I thought he might have known what that lady's name was or what some of the older men. I mean They played for years and years. So they were long established players. And you don't know why they let this one woman in, except she was male, I think? Yeah. And she played well. That was before I started playing, and went on after I went off to college. So... Well, that's an interesting story. Thank you so much. As I said, it was the city band. It wasn't just for the park, but I don't know anything of its earlier history. Well, that's certainly something that people talk about. Yes. Well, thank you, Julie. They went to listen to the band. Okay, thank you very much. You

lines.line: # Introduction and Background

> The interview begins with an introduction to Julie Kreisenseck, who was born in Boise in 1925. The setting is the Congregational Church in Boise. Julie shares her family’s historical ties to Boise, with her father being born there in 1884.
** introduction, Julie Kreisenseck, birth, Boise, family history **
keywords: ** introduction, Julie Kreisenseck, birth, Boise, family history **

metadata.title: Introduction and Background
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with an introduction to Julie Kreisenseck, who was born in Boise in 1925. The setting is the Congregational Church in Boise. Julie shares her family’s historical ties to Boise, with her father being born there in 1884.
metadata.keywords: introduction, Julie Kreisenseck, birth, Boise, family history

lines.line: # Julia Davis Park in Childhood

> Julie recalls her first memories of Julia Davis Park, mentioning the lagoon, swings, and activities during Depression times. She describes outings with family, special occasions when they could go boating, and the dynamics of going to the park during different seasons.
** Julia Davis Park, childhood, lagoon, swings, Depression, boating, seasons **
keywords: ** Julia Davis Park, childhood, lagoon, swings, Depression, boating, seasons **

metadata.title: Julia Davis Park in Childhood
metadata.synopsis: Julie recalls her first memories of Julia Davis Park, mentioning the lagoon, swings, and activities during Depression times. She describes outings with family, special occasions when they could go boating, and the dynamics of going to the park during different seasons.
metadata.keywords: Julia Davis Park, childhood, lagoon, swings, Depression, boating, seasons

lines.line: # Residential History and Park Visits

> Julie recounts her childhood residences and how visiting the park wasn't an everyday activity due to distance. She reflects on going to the park only with family and shares memories of picnicking and listening to the city band.
** residence, park visits, family outings, picnic, city band **
keywords: ** residence, park visits, family outings, picnic, city band **

metadata.title: Residential History and Park Visits
metadata.synopsis: Julie recounts her childhood residences and how visiting the park wasn't an everyday activity due to distance. She reflects on going to the park only with family and shares memories of picnicking and listening to the city band.
metadata.keywords: residence, park visits, family outings, picnic, city band

lines.line: # The City Band and Gender Dynamics

> The interview delves into Julie’s involvement with the city band during World War II. Initially, women were not allowed in the band, but necessity during the war opened opportunities for Julie and other women. Her cousin Jim Hopper’s involvement in music is also highlighted.
** city band, World War II, women, gender dynamics, Jim Hopper **
keywords: ** city band, World War II, women, gender dynamics, Jim Hopper **

metadata.title: The City Band and Gender Dynamics
metadata.synopsis: The interview delves into Julie’s involvement with the city band during World War II. Initially, women were not allowed in the band, but necessity during the war opened opportunities for Julie and other women. Her cousin Jim Hopper’s involvement in music is also highlighted.
metadata.keywords: city band, World War II, women, gender dynamics, Jim Hopper

lines.line: # Recollections of Playing in the Band

> Julie shares her experiences playing in the band during WWII, their performances, and her joy in participating at rodeos. Despite the challenges, she felt welcomed by her male counterparts in the band.
** band experiences, WWII, performances, rodeos, gender integration **
keywords: ** band experiences, WWII, performances, rodeos, gender integration **

metadata.title: Recollections of Playing in the Band
metadata.synopsis: Julie shares her experiences playing in the band during WWII, their performances, and her joy in participating at rodeos. Despite the challenges, she felt welcomed by her male counterparts in the band.
metadata.keywords: band experiences, WWII, performances, rodeos, gender integration

lines.line: # Family Life and Musical Inclusion

> The conversation turns to Julie's life post-high school, marriage, and returning to Boise in 1953. Her continued connection to the park, through her children and events like Art in the Park, is discussed.
** family, marriage, music, return to Boise, Art in the Park **
keywords: ** family, marriage, music, return to Boise, Art in the Park **

metadata.title: Family Life and Musical Inclusion
metadata.synopsis: The conversation turns to Julie's life post-high school, marriage, and returning to Boise in 1953. Her continued connection to the park, through her children and events like Art in the Park, is discussed.
metadata.keywords: family, marriage, music, return to Boise, Art in the Park

lines.line: # Changes in Park and River Access

> Julie reflects on the changes in the park and its relationship with the river over the years, how access and usage of the space have evolved, and mentions other social activities tied to locations like the Depot.
** park changes, river access, Depot, social activities **
keywords: ** park changes, river access, Depot, social activities **

metadata.title: Changes in Park and River Access
metadata.synopsis: Julie reflects on the changes in the park and its relationship with the river over the years, how access and usage of the space have evolved, and mentions other social activities tied to locations like the Depot.

lines.line: # Recreational and Social Dynamics

> Social dynamics of children interacting with the park and river are explored, with reflections on gender roles in recreational activities. Julie notes that activities like fishing and roaming were more prevalent among boys.
** recreational activities, gender roles, fishing, children **
keywords: ** recreational activities, gender roles, fishing, children **

lines.line: # Park Events and Legacy

> Julie discusses her daughter's participation in events at the park, maintaining a family tradition of using and enjoying the public space through multiple generations, and her memories of the city band.
** park events, family legacy, multiple generations, city band **
